narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 56.3% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make such decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, and background details like your lower-middle family income and plans to attend full-time, without considering gender or race. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the average of about 6 for students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a potential struggle in later undergraduate performance that often correlates with bar success. In contrast, your LSAT score of 35.5 worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since it's close to the 37.3 average among those who succeeded, showing your aptitude in that key area. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.4 provided a positive boost, exceeding the 3.24 average for bar passers and highlighting solid prior academic strength. While your full-time enrollment aligns with the vast majority of successful students (over 93%), and your family income bracket is represented among passers though less commonly than higher ones, these didn't outweigh the dominant signal from your Year 3 decile. Compared to typical accepted applicants—who often have stronger mid-to-late undergraduate deciles around 6, paired with LSATs and GPAs like yours or better—your profile stood out as riskier primarily due to that one key performance dip. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building experience to improve your later-year rankings, perhaps through targeted LSAT prep if you aim higher, or exploring part-time options that have worked for some. You might also strengthen your application with letters emphasizing your resilience from your circumstances. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these gaps—reapplying after bolstering your record could shift the odds significantly. Keep pushing; your strengths in GPA and LSAT show you're capable.
